sources: Ligia Alvarenga (Head of Procurement), 21 Aug 2025 and 10 Sep 2025 calls
metrics.narrative: Business problem is confirmed: lack of end-to-end visibility and integration from sourcing/negotiation through to payment is the top pain. Eduardo stated 'the lack of connections between negotiations and payments... is a black box, total black box... the weakness is high' (18 Jun 2026). Quantified organizational context: Nubank operates in Brazil, Mexico, Colombia with 135M customers, 7B BRL (~$1.4B USD) annual procurement spend, and ~20-person procurement team (Ligia, 21 Aug 2025). Pain quantified qualitatively but not in hard savings/ROI terms yet. Ligia described 'integration challenges... risk stemming from lack of connection between legal contracts, budget management, and payment systems' and 'manual integrations lack AI-driven insights for supplier recommendations, risk assessment, and budget matching' (10 Sep 2025 demo). Eduardo emphasized 'we don't have operational contract concept... value on top of contract expiry... no connection' (18 Jun). Buyer has not quantified cost of manual reconciliation, payment error rate, or savings target from automation. Next move: co-author CFO-ready ROI model quantifying current cost of disconnected systems (manual FTE hours, payment error/leakage rate, contract leakage) vs Zycus automation (cycle time reduction, FTE redeployment, 5-10% spend under management lift) to convert qualitative pain into EB-ready business case.

champion.narrative: Champion status is developing and at risk. Ligia Alvarenga (Head of Procurement, drove Aug-Sep 2025 discovery/demos) was the early advocate but has not appeared on calls since Eduardo Freitas (new EB) hired (~Mar 2026, 6+ month gap). Ligia's current role, reporting line, and advocacy are unknown. She demonstrated strong engagement in Aug-Sep 2025: led discovery call 21 Aug 2025, articulated pain ('integration challenges... lack of connection between contracts, budget, payments'), walked through detailed process flow (Jira → Netflix → Ariba → SAP), requested customer benchmarks and Horizon conference sponsorship, and asked for tailored demos (21 Aug, 10 Sep 2025). Natallia Pais (Legal Manager, governance/risk/A/P lead) and Giulliana Canesin (Legal Operations Specialist) are engaged influencers but have no EB access or deal-driving power; they asked detailed technical questions (AI clause analysis, supplier risk monitoring, budget checks, SLA tracking) in Aug-Sep 2025 demos but have not re-engaged since Eduardo hire. Eduardo (EB) is new (3 months tenure, first Zycus call 18 Jun 2026) and is evaluating Zycus alongside Coupa, Ariba, and build-in-house options; he is receptive but not an advocate. No coach, internal advocate, or multi-threaded champion exists. Risk: deal is single-threaded through Eduardo; if Eduardo exits, delays, or pivots, deal stalls with no internal advocate to restart momentum. Next move: reconnect with Ligia and offer to co-author CFO-ready ROI model she can present to Eduardo/CFO as her strategic value-add, converting her into active coach and rebuilding the Aug-Sep 2025 advocacy thread.

sources: Ligia Alvarenga and Natallia Pais, 10 Sep 2025 call
competition.narrative: Competitive landscape is confirmed and holistic. Three active competitors: (1) SAP Ariba (incumbent for supplier onboarding/qualification, highest current threat), (2) Coupa (peer vendor in RFI process), (3) Build in-house (parallel evaluation for certain modules). SAP Ariba is incumbent but buyer sentiment is strongly negative. Eduardo: 'we are using Ariba... quite honestly we receive a lot of complaints. Ariba is a difficult tool to customize... the culture of new bank is super agile, entrepreneur, fast mindset, avoid bureaucracy... when we have to implement S2P old school, difficult, super allergic, organization will not adapt to a tool, it should be the opposite' (18 Jun 2026). Ligia and Natallia confirmed same pain: 'Ariba... poor user experience, low adoption due to bureaucracy' (10 Sep 2025 notes). Status: Ariba is being replaced (RFI issued for iSupplier/iRisk replacement, 15 Jun 2026), so incumbent is DECLINING but still owns supplier onboarding today. Coupa is named peer competitor. Eduardo: 'I realized hey I need to implement here maybe a Coupa or an Ariba' then reconsidered due to the relevant stakeholder build-vs-buy mindset (18 Jun 2026). Karson positioned Zycus vs Coupa in same Gartner Magic Quadrant top-right (18 Jun call). Coupa status: ACTIVE in RFI/shortlist process, peer threat. Build in-house is active parallel decision. Eduardo: 'this whole mindset in Silicon Valley software disruption... easier to create your own software... thinking more about build internally our process or solutions' BUT confirmed 'we decided not to develop eSourcing and eRequest in-house, opting to acquire market solutions' (18 Jun 2026). Modules at risk of internal build: iContract, iRisk, Merlin intake orchestration. Confirmed buy-from-market: iSupplier (RFI active), iSource, eRequest. Status: build-in-house is ACTIVE for some modules, FADED for iSupplier/iSource. How we win: Zycus positioned as single-codebase S2P (vs Ariba/Coupa acquisition-based), Merlin AI core with autonomous negotiation agent (vs Coupa AI bolt-ons), built-in Merlin intake orchestration (vs Ariba clunky UX), 50%+ R&D investment (nimble, privately held), ReadyConnectors for SAP/Jira/Slack (seamless integration vs custom-code for build-in-house), and speed-to-value (3-6 months Zycus SaaS vs 12-18 months internal build). Wedge: prove Zycus can deliver tailored end-to-end orchestration (Jira intake → iSupplier → iSource → iContract → SAP payment) faster and cheaper than internal build, with better UX than Ariba, and deeper AI than Coupa. Activation: provide sandbox/POC to prove orchestration depth, LATAM references (Brazil/Mexico case studies), and modular staged-rollout pricing with ROI breakeven timeline to lock transparent pricing as differentiator vs Coupa. Buyer reception: Eduardo asked practical questions on SaaS pricing, module metrics, agent ownership, regional presence (18 Jun), signaling genuine evaluation vs reflexive buy.

sources: Ligia Alvarenga and Natallia Pais, 21 Aug 2025 and 10 Sep 2025 calls
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed and clearly articulated: lack of end-to-end visibility and integration from sourcing/negotiation through to contract execution and payment creates a 'black box' that exposes Nubank to operational risk, payment leakage, and budget overruns. Eduardo (EB) stated: 'the connection between negotiations and payments... is a black box, total black box... the lack of connections between that amount of value it's a black box... we don't have operational contract concept... the value is on top of the contract expiry... we have no connection. This is something that concerns me a bit' (18 Jun 2026). Pain owner is Eduardo Freitas, Procurement Director, who joined 3 months prior (Mar 2026) with mandate to professionalize procurement and implement end-to-end S2P tooling. Ligia (previous Head of Procurement, reported to FP&A director before Eduardo hire) and Natallia (governance, risk, A/P lead) articulated the same pain in Aug-Sep 2025 discovery: 'integration challenges... risk stemming from lack of connection between legal contracts, budget management, and payment systems' (Ligia, 10 Sep 2025); 'manual integrations lack AI-driven insights for supplier recommendations, risk assessment, and budget matching during contract and negotiation approvals' (Natallia, 10 Sep 2025); 'discrepancies between what requesters input as budget and eventual contract amounts create gap that may result in oversight of cost evaluations and risks' (Natallia, 21 Aug 2025). Secondary pain: low procurement maturity, largely tactical function, many tickets negotiated by user areas not centralized procurement, and current tooling (Ariba) is 'difficult to customize... bureaucratic... super allergic to organization' culture (Eduardo, 18 Jun). Quantified context: 7B BRL (~$1.4B USD) annual spend, ~20-person procurement team, 135M customers across Brazil/Mexico/Colombia, regulated fintech (18 Jun, 21 Aug 2025). Pain is confirmed but not yet quantified in hard savings/ROI terms (no cost of manual reconciliation, payment error rate, or automation savings target stated). Next move: co-author CFO-ready ROI model quantifying current cost of disconnected systems vs Zycus automation.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are standard SaaS MSA/SOW model. Eduardo confirmed Zycus model is 'Saas license... annual recurring cost... onetime fixed fee for implementation' and clarified code/agent ownership stays with Zycus ('it would still be on Zycus technology'), not transferred to buyer (18 Jun 2026). Nubank's internal contract approval process for vendor agreements: department lead approval by cost center, then negotiation approval by procurement manager/head or F&A head depending on threshold, then legal review, tax review, infosec, data protection, and signature via DocuSign (Natallia, 21 Aug 2025). For $100k SaaS deal, Eduardo (Procurement Director) likely owns selection but CFO may co-sign (threshold unconfirmed). Legal/InfoSec review timeline unknown; Nubank is regulated financial services (135M customers, Brazil/Mexico/Colombia ops) so InfoSec and data-protection review are expected gates. No SI/partner involvement mentioned. No MSA redline SLA, standard template, or contracting forcing function (e.g., existing contract expiration) stated. Next move: request standard vendor contract template and MSA redline SLA from Eduardo to prep legal review in parallel with RFP, and ask 'What is your standard legal/InfoSec review timeline for SaaS contracts? Any data-residency or regulatory requirements we should prep for (Brazil LGPD, Mexico data laws)?'

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Eduardo Freitas, Procurement Director, eduardo.freitas@nubank.com.br
economic_buyer.narrative: Eduardo Freitas, Procurement Director, is the identified Economic Buyer. He joined Nubank 3 months prior to 18 Jun 2026 call (~Mar 2026) and reports directly to Nubank CFO. Eduardo stated 'I joined 3 months ago as procurement director... new bank decided to have a proper director for procurement' and 'my boss, the CFO of New Bank, shared me an invitation for your event' (18 Jun 2026). Eduardo controls vendor selection and RFI process: 'RFI that we ask you is the only one we already defined to go to the market... we are going to engage in the next phase with one of the vendors' (18 Jun). Eduardo drove 18 Jun discovery call, asked detailed pricing/business-model questions ('what is the business model behind any of those modules? Saas by volume, fixed fee?'), and explored build-vs-buy tradeoffs. CFO (Eduardo's manager, name unknown) forwarded Horizon event invite to Eduardo, signaling awareness, but CFO has not appeared on calls and current sentiment/sponsorship are unknown. Eduardo's approval threshold and whether CFO co-signs contracts above a certain ACV are unconfirmed. Risk: deal is single-threaded through Eduardo (new, 3 months tenure); if Eduardo's sign-off ceiling is below $100k (six-module deal), CFO must co-sign, and we have no CFO relationship. Next move: validate Eduardo's approval authority ('What is your sign-off threshold for SaaS contracts? Does CFO co-sign above a certain ACV?') and request intro to CFO to create second exec thread and confirm executive sponsorship.

decision_process.narrative: Decision process is RFI → shortlist → RFP → vendor selection → pricing → EB/CFO sign-off → legal/MSA/SOW → contracting. Eduardo confirmed: 'RFI ongoing is the only one we already defined to go to the market... compiling answers to get a short list... we are going to engage in the next phase with one of the vendors' (18 Jun 2026). Next_Step progression: 11 Jun 2026 'received RFI with submission 6.15. once downselected will move to formal RFP'; 15 Jun 2026 'rfi submitted'; 29 Jun 2026 'fu on RFI and timelines'. Buyer is currently in RFI compilation/shortlist phase (as of 29 Jun 2026). Eduardo stated timeline expectation: 'initial demonstrations within this quarter, then Q4 shortlist a couple suppliers and dive deeper into their platforms' (10 Sep 2025 call context carried forward). Call sequence shows evaluation cadence: discovery 21 Aug 2025 (Ligia), deeper demo 10 Sep 2025 (Ligia, Natallia, Giulliana), full demo 26 Sep 2025, then 8-month gap until Eduardo re-engaged 18 Jun 2026 (new EB hire, first Zycus call). Eduardo controls vendor shortlist and RFP round. CFO (Eduardo's manager) must approve final contract (approval threshold unconfirmed). Legal/procurement approval chain: Eduardo stated Nubank has 'delegation flow for contracts below 50k... amounts above threshold have new negotiations, renewals... department lead approval... negotiation approval depending on threshold... procurement manager or head of procurement or head of F&A' (Natallia, 21 Aug 2025). For $100k six-module SaaS deal, Eduardo likely owns selection but CFO may co-sign. No SI/partner involvement mentioned. Contract mechanics: Eduardo asked 'do you have a model that after a period the agent and code is mine and legacy?' and Karson clarified 'it would still be on Zycus technology... Saas license model' (18 Jun), confirming standard SaaS MSA/SOW, not custom-build transfer. Next move: validate Eduardo's approval threshold and request CFO intro to create second exec thread.

sources: Natallia Pais and Ligia Alvarenga, 10 Sep 2025 call
decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are well-documented from discovery and demo sessions. Top-3 criteria: (1) End-to-end integration depth (Jira intake → iSupplier onboarding/risk → iSource RFQ → iContract negotiation/approval → SAP payment backend) to eliminate 'black box' between contracts and payments (Eduardo 18 Jun, Natallia 10 Sep, Ligia 21 Aug). Eduardo: 'lack of connection between negotiations and payments is a black box... should be a normal problem but in this situation as we don't have end-to-end tool the weakness is high' (18 Jun). (2) User-friendly, highly configurable, low-bureaucracy UX to fit Nubank's 'agile, entrepreneur, fast mindset' culture. Eduardo: 'Ariba is difficult to customize... culture is super agile... avoid bureaucracy... organization will not adapt to a tool, it should be the opposite' (18 Jun). Ligia: 'we felt frustration when we see specific presentations about long process and are not able to connect ourselves with the process' (10 Sep). (3) AI-driven automation and orchestration for supplier recommendations, risk assessment, budget matching, autonomous sourcing/negotiation. Eduardo: 'organization-wide AI transformation started March... spend analysis is one prompt... this is magic... super exciting' (18 Jun). Natallia: 'current systems lack AI intelligence to connect outputs across procurement phases including risk evaluations and supplier recommendations' (10 Sep). Secondary criteria: modular SaaS pricing with staged rollout (Eduardo: 'start with key challenge, see ROI, roll out other phases'; 18 Jun), LATAM regional presence and references (Eduardo: 'do you have presence in Brazil and Mexico companies?'; 18 Jun), speed-to-value vs internal build (Eduardo: 'thinking more about build internally'; 18 Jun). Criteria validation: Zycus positioned single-codebase S2P, Merlin AI core, built-in Merlin intake orchestration, autonomous negotiation agent, ReadyConnectors for SAP/Jira/Slack, and modular approach. Buyer reaction was receptive: Eduardo asked practical questions on SaaS pricing, module metrics, agent ownership, regional references (18 Jun). Next move: prepare hyper-tailored RFP demo mapped 1:1 to Nubank's Jira → Ariba → Netflix → SAP workflow (shown 21 Aug 2025) to prove Zycus 'gets it' and can replace entire disjointed stack.

Provide side-by-side TCO model (Zycus SaaS OpEx + R&D vs Coupa licensing + ongoing maintenance) and LATAM case studies to validate regional support vs Coupa.

Close date 21 Aug 2026 (56 days out) is unrealistic for the remaining decision path (RFI shortlist → RFP round → vendor selection → pricing → EB/CFO sign-off → legal/MSA/SOW/InfoSec review → contracting). Conservative timeline: 10-14 weeks, implying Oct-Nov close. Minimal slip to Q4 is likely. Risk: if Nubank's internal budget/fiscal year deadline is tied to the Aug close date (e.g., H2 2026 budget window closes end-Aug), the deal may be delayed to 2027 or Eduardo may accelerate by eliminating vendors or narrowing scope. Before RFP, ask Eduardo: 'Is the Aug 21 close date tied to a budget or fiscal deadline? If so, what is the latest date we can contract to hit that window?' and propose a fast-track modular approach (Phase 1: iSupplier + iSource by Aug; Phase 2: iContract + iRisk + Merlin + ANA by Q4) to meet budget deadline with staged delivery.

Regional validation gap: Eduardo asked 'do you have presence in Brazil and Mexico companies?' (18 Jun 2026), signaling regional capability and references are a decision criterion. Zycus has LATAM customers (Grupo Mariposa, Central America Bottling, Dacero, Nissan Mexico) but no Brazil-headquartered reference yet. Before RFP shortlist (~2 weeks, early Jul 2026), provide 2-3 LATAM reference customers (ideally financial services or high-volume procurement) with published case studies (savings KPIs, cycle-time reduction) and offer a reference call with a Brazil- or Mexico-based customer to validate regional support and cultural fit. If no Brazil reference exists, position Central America Bottling (Guatemala, same language/culture) and emphasize Zycus's ReadyConnectors for SAP (Nubank's payment backend) and partnership with PwC (LATAM presence) as regional support proof. Failing to validate regional capability before shortlist gives Coupa or a bundled SAP play (with deeper LATAM footprint) a wedge to exploit.

Champion gap: deal is single-threaded through Eduardo (new EB, 3 months tenure, first Zycus call 18 Jun 2026). Ligia (early advocate, drove Aug-Sep 2025 discovery/demos) has not reappeared since Sep 2025 (6+ months) after Eduardo was hired; her current role and advocacy are unknown. Natallia and Giulliana are engaged influencers but not champions with EB access or deal-driving power. No coach, internal advocate, or multi-threaded champion exists. Single-thread risk: if Eduardo exits, delays, or pivots to build-in-house, the deal stalls. Before RFP (2 weeks out), execute two moves: (1) Reconnect with Ligia (if still at Nubank) — offer to co-author a CFO-ready ROI model (anchored to $7B spend, six-module scope, quantified cycle-time/savings targets) she can present to Eduardo/CFO as her strategic value-add, converting her back into an active coach. (2) Request an intro to Eduardo's manager (CFO, who forwarded the Horizon invite) or a peer exec (CTO, if IT owns SaaS vendor decisions) under the guise of 'validating integration architecture with IT leadership' or 'confirming executive sponsorship for a $100k+ multi-module SaaS investment,' creating a second thread and reducing single-point-of-failure risk. Multi-threading before RFP is the highest-leverage move to secure advocacy and competitive intel.

Build-vs-buy threat: Eduardo stated 'modules... we are going to probably build in-house because we think in house it's better... we can have more control about customization' (18 Jun 2026), and confirmed eSourcing/eRequest will be bought from market but other modules (iContract, iRisk, orchestration) may be built in-house. Build-vs-buy is a parallel decision running alongside the RFI. Before RFP, proactively address the build case in the response and finalist demo: (1) Quantify time-to-value advantage (Zycus delivers 3-6 months vs 12-18 months internal build) and total cost of ownership (SaaS predictable OpEx + continuous R&D innovation vs internal build ongoing dev/maintenance headcount + distraction from core fintech product). (2) Emphasize Merlin AI depth (autonomous negotiation, supplier risk, one-prompt spend visibility) took years and 50%+ R&D to build; replicating internally is multi-year high-risk bet. (3) Highlight pre-built SAP/Ariba/Jira/Slack ReadyConnectors (vs custom-code every integration for internal build). (4) Offer sandbox/POC (Ligia requested 26 Sep 2025) to prove Zycus can deliver tailored orchestration workflow faster and cheaper than internal build. (5) Position AppExtend co-innovation model as middle path: speed of SaaS + custom flexibility without cost/risk of full build. Make the business case that buying Zycus modular SaaS for all six modules (iSupplier, iSource, iContract, iRisk, Merlin, ANA) is faster, lower-risk, and higher-ROI than building 3-4 of them in-house, especially given the 56-day close date pressure (though unrealistic, it signals urgency). If Eduardo pushes build for iContract or iRisk at RFP stage, be ready to negotiate a hybrid: Zycus delivers iSupplier + iSource + Merlin intake immediately, and Nubank evaluates build-vs-buy for iContract/iRisk in Phase 2 after seeing Zycus speed-to-value in Phase 1.

value.quote: Ariba is a difficult tool to customize, and the culture of new bank is super agile and entrepreneur and fast mindset, avoid bureaucracy... quite honestly, we receive a lot of complaints

value.name: Build in-house
value.quote: This whole mindset in Silicon Valley software disruption... easier to create your own software... myself as learner thinking more about build internally our process or solutions... BUT we decided not to develop eSourcing and eRequest in-house, opting to acquire market solutions while balancing price, features, and integration
value.source: Eduardo Freitas, 18 Jun 2026 call
value.sentiment: neutral
value.how_we_win: Quantify speed-to-value and TCO advantage of Zycus SaaS vs internal build: Zycus 3-6 months deploy + continuous R&D (50%+ workforce, Merlin AI depth, years of procurement domain expertise) vs 12-18 months internal build + ongoing dev/maintenance + distraction from core fintech product. Highlight pre-built SAP/Jira/Slack ReadyConnectors (vs custom-code integration cost/risk for internal build), Merlin AI orchestration (hard to replicate internally), and autonomous negotiation agent (years of R&D, market-tested). Offer sandbox/POC to prove Zycus can deliver tailored Jira → iSupplier → iSource → iContract → SAP orchestration faster and cheaper than internal build. Position AppExtend co-innovation as middle path: SaaS speed + custom flexibility without build cost/risk. Target modules at risk (iContract, iRisk, Merlin intake) and prove Zycus is better build partner than internal team.

value.name: Build in-house
value.quote: Eduardo stated 'modules... we are going to probably build in-house because we think in house it's better... we can have more control about customization' on 18 Jun 2026
value.source: Eduardo 18 Jun 2026 call
value.sentiment: negative
value.how_we_win: Quantify speed-to-value (Zycus 3-6 months vs 12-18 months internal build), TCO (SaaS OpEx vs ongoing dev/maintenance + distraction from core fintech product), Merlin AI depth (years of R&D, hard to replicate), and pre-built SAP/Jira/Slack integrations (vs custom-code for build). Offer sandbox/POC to prove Zycus delivers tailored orchestration faster and cheaper. Position AppExtend co-innovation as middle path: SaaS speed + custom flexibility without build cost/risk.

value.name: Build in-house
value.quote: modules... we are going to probably build in-house because we think in house it's a better you know, we can have more control about customization... we are not going to build eSourcing, eRequest... we should get one solution from the market
value.source: Requalification call 18 Jun 2026, Eduardo Freitas
value.sentiment: neutral
value.how_we_win: Eduardo confirmed eSourcing/eRequest (iSource) will be bought from market, so iSource is safe. Build-vs-buy is a parallel decision for iContract, iRisk, and orchestration/intake. Counter the build case with: (1) time-to-value — a market SaaS solution (Zycus) delivers in 3-6 months vs 12-18 months for internal build, and the 56-day close date pressure argues for speed; (2) total cost of ownership — internal build requires ongoing dev/maintenance/support headcount, whereas SaaS is predictable OpEx with continuous R&D innovation included; (3) risk of distraction — Nubank's core competency is fintech/banking, not procurement SaaS; building procurement software diverts eng resources from revenue-generating product; (4) AI depth — Merlin AI (autonomous negotiation, supplier risk, one-prompt spend visibility) took Zycus years and 50%+ R&D investment to build; replicating it internally is a multi-year, high-risk bet; (5) integration complexity — Zycus has pre-built SAP/Ariba/Jira/Slack connectors (ReadyConnectors), whereas internal build must custom-code every integration. Emphasize Zycus's co-innovation model (privately held, agile, open to custom builds via AppExtend if truly needed) as a middle path: speed of SaaS + flexibility of custom, without the cost/risk of full internal build. If Eduardo pushes build-vs-buy to final decision, offer a modular proof-of-concept (POC) or sandbox (Ligia requested this 26 Sep 2025) to show Zycus can deliver the tailored orchestration workflow faster and cheaper than internal build.

value.flag: Regional validation gap: Eduardo asked 'do you have presence in Brazil and Mexico companies?' (18 Jun 2026), signaling regional capability and references are a decision criterion. Zycus has LATAM customers (Grupo Mariposa, Central America Bottling, Dacero, Nissan Mexico) but no Brazil-headquartered reference yet. Before RFP shortlist (~2 weeks, early Jul 2026), provide 2-3 LATAM reference customers (ideally financial services or high-volume procurement) with published case studies (savings KPIs, cycle-time reduction) and offer a reference call with a Brazil- or Mexico-based customer to validate regional support and cultural fit. If no Brazil reference exists, position Central America Bottling (Guatemala, same language/culture) and emphasize Zycus's ReadyConnectors for SAP (Nubank's payment backend) and partnership with PwC (LATAM presence) as regional support proof. Failing to validate regional capability before shortlist gives Coupa or a bundled SAP play (with deeper LATAM footprint) a wedge to exploit.

value.flag: Build-vs-buy threat: Eduardo stated 'modules... we are going to probably build in-house because we think in house it's better... we can have more control about customization' (18 Jun 2026), and confirmed eSourcing/eRequest will be bought from market but other modules (iContract, iRisk, orchestration) may be built in-house. Build-vs-buy is a parallel decision running alongside the RFI. Before RFP, proactively address the build case in the response and finalist demo: (1) Quantify time-to-value advantage (Zycus delivers 3-6 months vs 12-18 months internal build) and total cost of ownership (SaaS predictable OpEx + continuous R&D innovation vs internal build ongoing dev/maintenance headcount + distraction from core fintech product). (2) Emphasize Merlin AI depth (autonomous negotiation, supplier risk, one-prompt spend visibility) took years and 50%+ R&D to build; replicating internally is multi-year high-risk bet. (3) Highlight pre-built SAP/Ariba/Jira/Slack ReadyConnectors (vs custom-code every integration for internal build). (4) Offer sandbox/POC (Ligia requested 26 Sep 2025) to prove Zycus can deliver tailored orchestration workflow faster and cheaper than internal build. (5) Position AppExtend co-innovation model as middle path: speed of SaaS + custom flexibility without cost/risk of full build. Make the business case that buying Zycus modular SaaS for all six modules (iSupplier, iSource, iContract, iRisk, Merlin, ANA) is faster, lower-risk, and higher-ROI than building 3-4 of them in-house, especially given the 56-day close date pressure (though unrealistic, it signals urgency). If Eduardo pushes build for iContract or iRisk at RFP stage, be ready to negotiate a hybrid: Zycus delivers iSupplier + iSource + Merlin intake immediately, and Nubank evaluates build-vs-buy for iContract/iRisk in Phase 2 after seeing Zycus speed-to-value in Phase 1.
